station.title: Jaw discomfort while carrying groceries
station.note: Original practice station aligned to the current AMC format. It is not an AMC examination station or official marking script.

lines.text: You are a general practitioner working in a community medical clinic.
Ms Mirella Quinn, a 62-year-old woman, has attended after becoming short of breath and nauseated while carrying groceries from her car. She also noticed an ache in her lower jaw.
No observations or investigation results are available at the start of the consultation.

tasks.text: Explain your most likely diagnosis and important differential diagnoses to the patient.
Address the patient's concerns and explain your management plan.

essentialClues.text: Exertional jaw discomfort, breathlessness and nausea without volunteered chest pain
Symptoms began with exertion and a previous exertional episode settled with rest
Diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, previous smoking and family history

discriminators.text: Ongoing exertional pressure with autonomic symptoms, without abrupt maximum intensity or pleuritic character

redFlags.text: Ongoing exertional symptoms with central heaviness and autonomic features

safetyThresholds.text: Suspected acute coronary syndrome in primary care requires prompt transfer for definitive assessment
Oxygen is reserved for oxygen saturation below 90 percent, heart failure or shock rather than given routinely
Ongoing chest discomfort warrants glyceryl trinitrate after blood pressure and contraindication checks
A normal first ECG cannot safely exclude acute coronary syndrome

criticalActions.text: Call Triple Zero promptly and use monitored ambulance transfer
Begin cardiac monitoring with defibrillator availability and arrange a 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes without delaying transfer
Give aspirin 300 milligrams chewed after excluding true anaphylaxis and active bleeding

criticalActionTimings.positiveActionPhrase: The doctor directs a clinic colleague to call Triple Zero
criticalActionTimings.rationale: The complete high-concern cardiac pattern is established before the remaining history is taken.
Ongoing ischaemic symptoms require monitoring and defibrillator availability during assessment.

criticalActionTimings.positiveActionPhrase: The doctor gives Mirella aspirin 300 milligrams to chew
criticalActionTimings.rationale: Aspirin is administered after the relevant contraindication checks and consent.

unsafeActions.text: Do not dismiss an ACS presentation because the patient does not volunteer chest pain
Do not use private transport for ongoing suspected acute coronary syndrome
Do not use one normal ECG to exclude acute coronary syndrome

scopeBoundary.text: The GP begins safe assessment and treatment while arranging prompt transfer; serial ECG and troponin assessment occur in hospital

initialVoluntary.text: About 25 minutes ago, while carrying groceries, I became breathless and sick, with a dull ache across my lower jaw. It has not settled.

concerns.text: Why an ambulance is needed without severe chest pain
concerns.responseText: An ambulance? I do not have real chest pain.

concerns.text: Whether a heart attack is definite
concerns.responseText: Does that mean I am definitely having a heart attack?

concerns.text: Whether her daughter can drive her
concerns.responseText: My daughter is outside. Could she take me straight to hospital instead?

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about current symptoms.
disclosures.responseText: Yes, I am still breathless and nauseated.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about chest discomfort.
disclosures.responseText: The middle feels heavy, but I did not think of it as pain.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about persistence.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about character.
disclosures.responseText: Like a tight band sitting behind my breastbone.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about time to maximum intensity.
disclosures.responseText: No. It built up over two or three minutes.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about response to rest.
disclosures.responseText: The jaw ache eased, but the heaviness and breathlessness continued.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about associated symptoms.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about previous episodes.
disclosures.responseText: Last week, carrying laundry upstairs caused a milder episode that settled with rest.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about pleuritic character.
disclosures.responseText: No, breathing does not change it.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about unilateral leg swelling.
disclosures.responseText: No, neither leg has been swollen.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about recent immobility.
disclosures.responseText: No, I have been moving around normally.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about medical history.
disclosures.responseText: Type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure and high cholesterol.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about regular medicines.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about severe aspirin allergy.
disclosures.responseText: No, I have never had a severe reaction to aspirin.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about active bleeding.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about anticoagulant use.
disclosures.responseText: No, I do not take a blood-thinning medicine.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about smoking history.
disclosures.responseText: I stopped eight years ago. Before that I smoked for about twenty years.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about family history.
disclosures.responseText: My mother had a heart attack at 61.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about understanding of the plan.
disclosures.responseText: You think this could be my heart even though the pain is not severe. I need to go by ambulance and have heart tests at hospital.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment.

scheduledPrompts.speaker: Examiner
scheduledPrompts.responseText: Four minutes have passed. Please proceed to the remaining tasks.
scheduledPrompts.markingConsequence: The Doctor proceeds to the remaining tasks; the prompt itself carries no performance inference.

closing.text: Thank you. The station is complete.

keySteps.description: Elicits the exertional jaw, breathlessness, nausea and central-pressure pattern
Recognises the danger threshold and activates ambulance transfer before diagnostic certainty
Explains acute coronary syndrome, uncertainty and important alternatives
Provides evidence-based initial primary-care management without adding a handover

projectSafetyErrors.description: Dismisses ACS because Mirella did not volunteer severe chest pain
Waits to finish the history or obtain a diagnostic test before transfer
Allows private transport despite ongoing suspected ACS

globalRating.decisionRule: The global rating alone determines the station result: ratings 1 to 3 fail and ratings 4 to 7 pass.
globalRating.passStandard: Completes the assigned tasks, recognises the non-classic dangerous cardiac pattern, explains uncertainty and provides safe initial management.
globalRating.failStandard: Misses the dangerous pattern, delays transfer, gives unsafe reassurance or substantially fails the assigned tasks.

identity.verificationText: Hello, I am Ushana, one of the doctors here. Could you confirm your full name and date of birth?
identity.rationale: The patient directly confirms full name and date of birth before any clinical question.

consents.requestText: Mirella, may I ask about what happened?
Mirella, may I give you the aspirin now?

informationRequests.requestText: Tell me what happened from the beginning.
informationRequests.responseText: About 25 minutes ago, while carrying groceries, I became breathless and sick, with a dull ache across my lower jaw. It has not settled.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Are you still feeling unwell now?
informationRequests.responseText: Yes, I am still breathless and nauseated.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you noticed any discomfort in your chest?
informationRequests.responseText: The middle feels heavy, but I did not think of it as pain.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Is the heaviness still present?
informationRequests.responseText: Yes, it is still there.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: What does the heaviness feel like?
informationRequests.responseText: Like a tight band sitting behind my breastbone.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Did it reach its worst point instantly?
informationRequests.responseText: No. It built up over two or three minutes.
responseEvidencePhrases: built up over two or three minutes
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: What happened after you stopped carrying the bags?
informationRequests.responseText: The jaw ache eased, but the heaviness and breathlessness continued.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: What other symptoms came with it?
informationRequests.responseText: I became clammy and light-headed.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you experienced anything similar before?
informationRequests.responseText: Last week, carrying laundry upstairs caused a milder episode that settled with rest.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Does taking a deep breath change the heaviness?
informationRequests.responseText: No, breathing does not change it.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you recently noticed swelling in one leg?
informationRequests.responseText: No, neither leg has been swollen.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you recently been immobile for a long period?
informationRequests.responseText: No, I have been moving around normally.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: What medical conditions do you have?
informationRequests.responseText: Type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure and high cholesterol.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: What regular medicines do you take?
informationRequests.responseText: Metformin, empagliflozin, telmisartan and atorvastatin.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you ever had a severe allergic reaction to aspirin?
informationRequests.responseText: No, I have never had a severe reaction to aspirin.
responseEvidencePhrases: never had a severe reaction to aspirin
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Are you bleeding anywhere now?
informationRequests.responseText: No, I am not bleeding.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Do you take any blood-thinning medicine?
informationRequests.responseText: No, I do not take a blood-thinning medicine.
responseEvidencePhrases: do not take a blood-thinning medicine
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Have you ever smoked?
informationRequests.responseText: I stopped eight years ago. Before that I smoked for about twenty years.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: Has a close relative had heart disease?
informationRequests.responseText: My mother had a heart attack at 61.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

informationRequests.requestText: What do you understand about why hospital assessment is needed?
informationRequests.responseText: You think this could be my heart even though the pain is not severe. I need to go by ambulance and have heart tests at hospital.
informationRequests.responseUse: The next Doctor turn uses this answer to continue the assigned task.

variation.distinctJob: Recognise possible myocardial ischaemia when exertional jaw discomfort, breathlessness and nausea are volunteered before central heaviness is elicited.
variation.newSurprise: Central chest heaviness is disclosed only after a focused question because the patient does not regard pressure as pain.
variation.plannedTrap: The absence of volunteered chest pain may falsely lower concern despite a coherent exertional and autonomic pattern.
antiFailureFeatures: The Doctor asks directly about chest discomfort without forcing the patient to call it pain.
Ambulance transfer begins once the dangerous pattern is established, before the remaining history is completed.

duplicateCheck.conclusion: This new case trains an atypical ACS presentation without volunteered chest pain. The Emergency Explore comparisons cover aortic dissection, pulmonary embolism and tension pneumothorax rather than this recognition task.

actionDurations.exceptionRationale: The reviewed ongoing cardiac pattern requires prompt ambulance activation and monitoring before diagnostic certainty.

requiredEvidence.requirement: A focused history is visibly initiated
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: Tell me what happened from the beginning.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: Tell me what happened from the beginning.
You have ongoing central heaviness, jaw discomfort, breathlessness, nausea and clamminess after exertion

requiredEvidence.requirement: The most likely diagnosis is explicitly explained
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: my main concern is acute coronary syndrome
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: my main concern is acute coronary syndrome

requiredEvidence.requirement: Important differential diagnoses are explicitly explained
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: Important differential diagnoses are a clot in the lung, a problem with the aorta and a collapsed lung.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: Important differential diagnoses are a clot in the lung, a problem with the aorta and a collapsed lung.

requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: The ambulance is already on the way and you have had aspirin.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: The ambulance is already on the way and you have had aspirin.

requiredEvidence.requirement: The patient's concern about private transport is answered
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: The ambulance allows monitoring and treatment during transport
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: The ambulance allows monitoring and treatment during transport

listenTest.notes: Timestamped read-through places the four-minute Examiner prompt after the focused-history summary at 240 seconds. The diagnostic explanation takes 75 seconds and the concerns plus management discussion takes 123 seconds, for a total of 438 seconds.

claimMap.claim: Exertional pressure, jaw discomfort, dyspnoea, nausea and sweating can represent acute coronary syndrome even when the patient does not volunteer chest pain; non-specific presentations occur more often in women and people with diabetes.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, pp. 26-27, history of presenting complaint

claimMap.claim: The absence of volunteered chest pain must not be used to dismiss acute coronary syndrome when exertional jaw discomfort, dyspnoea and autonomic symptoms form a concerning pattern.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, pp. 26-27, history of presenting complaint and accompanying symptoms
Clinical presentation section, non-specific symptoms in women and people with diabetes

claimMap.claim: Important time-critical alternatives include acute aortic syndrome, pulmonary embolism and pneumothorax; symptom character and relevant risk features guide discrimination without safely excluding disease from one negative answer.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, pp. 26-27, targeted differential diagnoses
Clinical presentation and differential assessment sections

claimMap.claim: Suspected acute coronary syndrome initially assessed in primary care requires prompt transfer to a facility able to perform definitive risk assessment.

Ongoing suspected ACS requires cardiac monitoring with defibrillator availability and a 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes; one normal ECG does not exclude NSTEACS and serial ECG plus troponin assessment is required.

sourceLocators.locator: Initial ECG assessment and primary-care presentation recommendations
ECG section: ECG may be normal in over 30% of NSTEACS

claimMap.claim: A single normal ECG does not exclude non-ST-elevation acute coronary syndrome; ongoing concern requires serial ECG and high-sensitivity troponin assessment.

sourceLocators.locator: Initial ECG assessment and serial testing recommendations
ECG section: an ECG may be normal in more than 30% of NSTEACS

claimMap.claim: Give aspirin 300 milligrams chewed or dissolved in suspected acute coronary syndrome unless contraindicated, after excluding true anaphylaxis and active bleeding.
Supplemental oxygen is not routinely required when oxygen saturation is at least 90 percent; give it for saturation below 90 percent, heart failure or shock, while avoiding hyperoxaemia.

Give sublingual glyceryl trinitrate for ongoing chest discomfort when blood pressure is adequate and no contraindication such as right ventricular infarction or recent phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor use is present.

sourceLocators.locator: Initial therapeutic management recommendation and nitrates practice points, pp. 35-36

claimMap.claim: The station uses the current AMC two-minute reading and eight-minute assessment format, predominant assessment area, observable tasks, domains and seven-point global rating threshold.

sourceLocators.locator: Clinical Examination Specifications V8.3, station structure, timing and task sections
Assessment Domains V1.1, domain definitions and performance expectations

taskAlignment.method: Task-to-line and task-evidence review including no-unrequested-handover control

examinerRelease.method: Examiner ownership and four-minute prompt review
